title: Two-Source Rail Build
learningObjectives: Stack sources safely to reach higher rail voltages
Adjust resistance to manage current at higher voltages
Keep power within safe bounds when voltage rises
introText: Higher rails need careful resistance. You'll stack two sources to reach a higher voltage, then raise resistance to control current and power.

steps.instructionText: Place two DC sources and a resistor in series, wired into a closed loop.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Loop is closed with both sources in series.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Wire both sources and the resistor end-to-end so the loop is complete.

steps.instructionText: Set each source near 9V so the total voltage is about 18V.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Total supply is about 18V — a higher rail for advanced loads.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Adjust both sources until the total voltage is close to 18V.

steps.instructionText: Increase resistance until power drops below 0.35 W.
steps.feedbackOnSuccess: Power is under 0.35 W, keeping the higher rail safe.
steps.feedbackOnFailure: Raise resistance further or lower voltage to reduce power.

questions.questionText: What risk increases when stacking sources for higher voltage?
options: Higher power that can overheat components
questions.explanation: Higher voltage can raise both current and power unless resistance is increased.

questions.questionText: How do you keep power safe at higher voltage?
questions.explanation: Increase resistance or decrease voltage so P = V × I stays within limits.
